case_name: Radium removal from brackish groundwater for recharge under high-recovery and concentrate limits
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of treatment process chains for radium-bearing brackish groundwater intended for groundwater recharge, balancing radionuclide compliance, salinity reduction, high water recovery, and difficult concentrate management constraints.

input_data.scenario_background: A water district is evaluating treatment of inland brackish groundwater for aquifer recharge to augment a stressed basin. The source contains elevated combined radium and salinity, so the project must both control radionuclides and lower dissolved solids enough to support recharge compatibility. However, inland concentrate disposal options are limited and expensive, making very low-recovery membrane schemes unattractive even if they are technically strong. The trade-off is therefore between simpler radium-focused treatment with less concentrate burden and membrane-based desalting that better addresses salinity but creates a residuals management challenge.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should achieve stable combined radium compliance and produce recharge-compatible salinity at a practicable overall water recovery.
secondary_goals: maximize water recovery because source development is limited
minimize volume of difficult inland concentrate or radioactive residuals requiring off-site management
avoid overly complex operation for a district-scale brackish groundwater facility
maintain robust performance despite moderate feedwater scaling tendency

engineering_constraints.residuals_management: concentrate disposal is a major constraint and radioactive solids handling should be minimized where possible
engineering_constraints.robustness_requirement: treatment train should tolerate moderate variability in salinity and scaling potential while sustaining high recovery

core_unit_functions.pretreatment_and_antiscalant: control particulate loading and scaling risk to support elevated membrane recovery
core_unit_functions.high_recovery_reverse_osmosis: reduce TDS substantially while also rejecting radium and associated divalent ions
core_unit_functions.lime_or_caustic_stabilization: adjust finished water chemistry for recharge compatibility and corrosion control
core_unit_functions.polishing_cartridge_filtration: provide final particulate control before recharge

applicability_rationale: this route directly addresses both key constraints in the dual-contaminant case because it lowers salinity and radium in one train
radium is strongly rejected by RO, and brackish groundwater desalting is a common fit when TDS reduction is also required
although concentrate handling is difficult, configuring the system for high recovery makes it more aligned with the project objective than lower-recovery membrane designs
moderate DOC and low turbidity favor membrane pretreatment requirements that are manageable at district scale
potential_risks: inland RO concentrate will contain elevated dissolved salts and radionuclides and requires a credible disposal strategy
high recovery increases scaling risk from sulfate, barium, strontium, and hardness and requires disciplined antiscalant and recovery control
if recovery is pushed too aggressively, operational instability or cleaning frequency may rise

core_unit_functions.pretreatment: remove suspended solids and protect downstream ion exchange and EDR equipment
core_unit_functions.cation_exchange_softening_for_radium: selectively remove radium along with hardness-causing cations
core_unit_functions.high_recovery_electrodialysis_reversal: reduce salinity at comparatively high water recovery with less scaling sensitivity than some membrane-only approaches
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust product water chemistry for recharge compatibility

applicability_rationale: this hybrid route is attractive when the project strongly values recovery and wants to moderate concentrate volume relative to conventional RO
radium can be effectively controlled by cation exchange, while EDR provides desalting that can be favorable for brackish waters with a high-recovery objective
EDR can be operationally resilient under scaling-prone waters when polarity reversal and pretreatment are well managed
the combination spreads treatment duty across two units, which can reduce radionuclide loading to the desalting stage
potential_risks: the process creates two residual streams to manage: spent regenerant or waste brine from radium removal and EDR concentrate
operational complexity is higher because both ion exchange performance and EDR stack condition must be managed
if radium removal by the front-end exchanger is inconsistent, downstream concentrate radioactivity management can become more difficult

core_unit_functions.pretreatment: prepare feed and support reliable clarification and downstream membrane performance
core_unit_functions.lime_soda_softening_with_clarification: co-precipitate radium with hardness removal and reduce scaling precursors
core_unit_functions.media_filtration: remove carryover solids from softening before membranes
core_unit_functions.high_recovery_reverse_osmosis: provide major TDS reduction and additional radium rejection
core_unit_functions.stabilization: restore suitable finished water chemistry for recharge

applicability_rationale: this train is technically robust because it uses established radium coprecipitation chemistry and then desalting for recharge salinity targets
front-end softening can improve RO recovery potential by reducing sulfate and hardness scaling pressure
it may be attractive where source-water scaling tendency is severe enough to justify more pretreatment
potential_risks: this option generates both radioactive softening sludge and RO concentrate, which is a poor match for stringent residuals constraints
chemical handling, sludge management, and membrane operation together increase operator burden
footprint is larger than simpler radium removal or standalone desalting alternatives

core_unit_functions.pretreatment: protect ion exchange resin from particulate fouling
core_unit_functions.cation_exchange_softening_for_radium: remove radium and hardness through ion exchange
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust chemistry after softening for recharge suitability

applicability_rationale: for radium alone, cation exchange is a recognized and often effective treatment option
this route avoids a large desalting concentrate stream and can be comparatively compact
it may be considered if recharge salinity criteria are flexible or if blending with lower-TDS water is available elsewhere in the project
potential_risks: it does not materially reduce TDS, so it is a weak fit when recharge compatibility requires substantial salinity reduction
regeneration waste or spent resin management still involves radionuclide-bearing residuals
sodium addition from softening exchange can further worsen recharge chemistry if not accounted for

core_unit_functions.pretreatment: prepare raw water for chemical softening
core_unit_functions.lime_soda_softening_with_clarification: remove radium by co-precipitation with hardness solids

applicability_rationale: lime softening is technically capable for radium removal and also lowers hardness and some scaling constituents
it can be relevant where membrane desalting is not feasible at all and the main requirement is radionuclide reduction
potential_risks: this option does not achieve the needed TDS reduction for a true brackish-water recharge application
radioactive sludge production is significant and directly conflicts with residuals management constraints
chemical consumption, solids handling, and clarifier control increase O&M burden

preferred_rank_pattern: processes that address both radium and salinity should rank above radium-only options
high-recovery desalting routes should be favored over conventional lower-recovery concepts because water recovery is a stated priority
technically capable radium-removal-only options should rank lower due to misalignment with recharge salinity goals
options producing multiple difficult residual streams should not rank first unless they offer a strong benefit on both contaminants

must_reflect: the inland concentrate handling limitation should materially penalize membrane-heavy options with poor residuals profiles
the high-water-recovery objective should materially favor EDR or optimized high-recovery RO over lower-recovery desalting concepts
dual-contaminant logic should distinguish radionuclide control from broader dissolved-solids control
residual radioactive sludge or regenerant handling should be considered alongside concentrate disposal

must_contain: why radium-only treatments are insufficient when recharge compatibility also requires salinity reduction
why RO or EDR are plausible because brackish groundwater has elevated TDS
why concentrate and radioactive residuals management change the ranking even when removal performance is high
why hardness, sulfate, barium, and strontium imply scaling and recovery trade-offs

should_mention: RO or EDR concentrate disposal difficulty
failure to meet recharge salinity objectives with radium-only treatment
